subject: Mathematics
q:
Let $\mathrm{x}_1, \mathrm{x}_2, \ldots \ldots, \mathrm{x}_{\mathrm{n}}$ be $\mathrm{n}$ observations, and let $\overline{\mathrm{x}}$ be their arithematic mean and $\sigma^2$ be their variance. 
Statement $1$: Variance of $2 x_1, 2 x_2, \ldots \ldots, 2 x_n$ is $4 \sigma^2$.
Statement $2$: Arithmetic mean of $2 \mathrm{x}_1, 2 \mathrm{x}_2, \ldots . ., 2 \mathrm{x}_{\mathrm{n}}$ is $4 \overline{\mathrm{x}}$.
question:
Let $\mathrm{x}_1, \mathrm{x}_2, \ldots \ldots, \mathrm{x}_{\mathrm{n}}$ be $\mathrm{n}$ observations, and let $\overline{\mathrm{x}}$ be their arithematic mean and $\sigma^2$ be their variance. 
Statement $1$: Variance of $2 x_1, 2 x_2, \ldots \ldots, 2 x_n$ is $4 \sigma^2$.
Statement $2$: Arithmetic mean of $2 \mathrm{x}_1, 2 \mathrm{x}_2, \ldots . ., 2 \mathrm{x}_{\mathrm{n}}$ is $4 \overline{\mathrm{x}}$.
options: Statement $1$ is false, statement $2$ is true
Statement $1$ is true, statement $2$ is true; statement $2$ is a correct explanation for statement $1$
Statement $1$ is true, statement $2$ is true; statement $2$ is not a correct explanation for statement $1$
Statement $1$ is true, statement $2$ is false
solution:
$\sigma^2=\sum \frac{x_i^2}{n}-\left(\sum \frac{x_i}{n}\right)^2$
Variance of $2 x_1, 2 x_2, \ldots . ., 2 x_n=\sum \frac{\left(2 x_i\right)^2}{n}-\left(\sum \frac{2 x_i}{n}\right)^2=4\left[\sum \frac{x_i^2}{n}-\left(\sum \frac{x_i}{n}\right)^2\right]=4 \sigma^2$ Statement $1$ is true.
A.M. of $2 x_1, 2 x_2, \ldots \ldots, 2 x_n=\frac{2 x_1+2 x_2+\cdots+2 x_n}{n}=2\left(\frac{x_1+x_2+\cdots+x_n}{n}\right)=2 \bar{x}$
Statement $2$ is false.

q: Let the mean and variance of four numbers <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>7</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>x</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>y</mi><mo> </mo><mo>(</mo><mi>x</mi><mo>></mo><mi>y</mi><mo>)</mo></math> be <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn></math> respectively. subject: Mathematics
q:
If the mean and the variance of the data
\(\begin{array}{|c|c|c|c|c|} \hline \text{Class} & 4\text{-}8 & 8\text{-}12 & 12\text{-}16 & 16\text{-}20 \\ \hline \text{Frequency} & 3 & \lambda & 4 & 7 \\ \hline \end{array}\)
are $\mu$ and 19 respectively, then the value of $\lambda+\mu$ is
question:
If the mean and the variance of the data
\(\begin{array}{|c|c|c|c|c|} \hline \text{Class} & 4\text{-}8 & 8\text{-}12 & 12\text{-}16 & 16\text{-}20 \\ \hline \text{Frequency} & 3 & \lambda & 4 & 7 \\ \hline \end{array}\)
are $\mu$ and 19 respectively, then the value of $\lambda+\mu$ is
solution:
Class midpoints: $6, 10, 14, 18$.
Frequencies: $3, \lambda, 4, 7$. $N = 14 + \lambda$.
Mean $\mu = \frac{200 + 10\lambda}{14 + \lambda}$.
$\sum f_i x_i^2 = 3(36) + 100\lambda + 4(196) + 7(324) = 3160 + 100\lambda$.
Variance: $19 = \frac{3160+100\lambda}{N} - \mu^2$.
Let $u = 14+\lambda$:
$19u^2 = u(1760+100u) - (60+10u)^2 \Rightarrow 19u^2 - 560u + 3600 = 0$.
$u = \frac{560 \pm 200}{38}$, so $u = 20 \Rightarrow \lambda = 6$.
$\mu = \frac{260}{20} = 13$. Hence $\lambda + \mu = 6 + 13 = 19$.

subject: Mathematics
q: Let $\mathrm{X}=\{x \in \mathrm{~N}: 1 \leq x \leq 19\}$ and for some $a, b \in \mathbb{R}, \mathrm{Y}=\{a x+b: x \in \mathrm{X}\}$. If the mean and variance of the elements of Y are 30 and 750, respectively, then the sum of all possible values of $b$ is
question: Let $\mathrm{X}=\{x \in \mathrm{~N}: 1 \leq x \leq 19\}$ and for some $a, b \in \mathbb{R}, \mathrm{Y}=\{a x+b: x \in \mathrm{X}\}$. If the mean and variance of the elements of Y are 30 and 750, respectively, then the sum of all possible values of $b$ is
solution:
Mean of $X = \frac{1+19}{2} = 10$, Variance of $X = \frac{19^2-1}{12} = 30$.
For $Y = aX + b$: Mean of $Y = 10a + b = 30$ and Variance of $Y = a^2 \cdot 30 = 750$.
$a^2 = 25 \Rightarrow a = 5$ or $a = -5$.
If $a = 5$: $b = 30 - 50 = -20$.
If $a = -5$: $b = 30 + 50 = 80$.
Sum of all possible values of $b = -20 + 80 = 60$.

subject: Mathematics
q: The median of 100 observations grouped in classes of equal width is 25 . If the median class interval is 20-30 and the number of observations less than 20 is 45 , then the frequency of median class is
question: The median of 100 observations grouped in classes of equal width is 25 . If the median class interval is 20-30 and the number of observations less than 20 is 45 , then the frequency of median class is
solution:
Median is given as
$$
M=l+\frac{\frac{N}{2}-F}{f} \times C
$$
where
$l=$ lower limit of the median - class
$f=$ frequency of the median class
$N=$ total frequency
$F=$ cumulative frequency of the class just before the median class
$C=$ length of median class
Now, given, $M=25, N=100, F=45$, $C=20-30=10, l=20$.
$\therefore$ By using formula, we have
$$
\begin{aligned}
& 25=20+\frac{50-45}{f} \times 10 \\
& 25-20=\frac{50}{f} \Rightarrow 5=\frac{50}{f} \Rightarrow f=10
\end{aligned}
$$

subject: Mathematics
q: The mean of the numbers $a, b, 8,5,10$ is 6 and the variance is $6.80$. Then which one of the following gives possible values of $a$ and $b$ ?
question: The mean of the numbers $a, b, 8,5,10$ is 6 and the variance is $6.80$. Then which one of the following gives possible values of $a$ and $b$ ?
solution:
Mean of $a, b, 8,5,10$ is 6
$$
\begin{aligned}
& \Rightarrow \frac{a+b+8+5+10}{5}=6 \\
& \Rightarrow a+b=7
\end{aligned}
$$
Given that Variance is $6.8$
$$
\begin{aligned}
& \therefore \text { Variance }=\frac{\sum\left(\mathrm{X}_{\mathrm{i}}-\mathrm{A}\right)^2}{\mathrm{n}} \\
& =\frac{(\mathrm{a}-6)^2+(\mathrm{b}-6)^2+4+1+16}{5}=6.8
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \Rightarrow a^2+b^2=25 \\
& a^2+(7-a)^2=25 \\
& \Rightarrow a^2-7 a+12=0 \\
& \therefore a=4,3 \text { and } b=3,4
\end{aligned}
$$

q: Let $\bar{X}$ and M.D. be the mean and the mean deviation about $\bar{X}$ of $n$ observations $\mathrm{x}_{\mathrm{i}}, \mathrm{i}=1,2$,n. If each of the observations is increased by 5 , then the new mean and the mean deviation about the new mean, respectively, are :
question: Let $\bar{X}$ and M.D. be the mean and the mean deviation about $\bar{X}$ of $n$ observations $\mathrm{x}_{\mathrm{i}}, \mathrm{i}=1,2$,n. If each of the observations is increased by 5 , then the new mean and the mean deviation about the new mean, respectively, are :
solution:
Let $x_i$ be $n$ observations, $i=1,2, \ldots n$ Let $\bar{X}$ be the mean and M.D be the mean deviation about $\bar{X}$. If each observation is increased by 5 then new mean will be $\bar{X}+5$ and new M.D. about new mean will be M.D.
$$
\left(\because \text { Mean }=\sum_{i=1}^n \frac{x_i}{n}\right)
$$

q: Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>n</mi></math> be an odd natural number such that the variance of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>2</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>3</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>4</mn><mo>,</mo><mo>…</mo><mo>,</mo><mi>n</mi></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>14</mn><mo>.</mo></math> Then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>n</mi></math> is equal to ________.
question: Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>n</mi></math> be an odd natural number such that the variance of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>2</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>3</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>4</mn><mo>,</mo><mo>…</mo><mo>,</mo><mi>n</mi></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>14</mn><mo>.</mo></math> Then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>n</mi></math> is equal to ________.
solution:
Variance of n natural number is given by,

Variance=∑n2n-∑nn2=nn+12n+16n-nn+12n2

                  =n+12n+16-n+14=n+1n-112=n2-112

And it is given that Variance=14

⇒n2-112=14

⇒n2-1=168

⇒n=13

q: If the data <math><msub><mrow><mi>x</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow></msub><mo>,</mo><msub><mrow><mi>x</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub><mo>,</mo><mo>…</mo><msub><mrow><mi>x</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>10</mn></mrow></msub></math> is such that the mean of first four of these is <math><mn>11</mn><mo>,</mo></math> the mean of the remaining six is <math><mn>16</mn></math> and the sum of squares of all of these is <math><mn>2000</mn></math>, then the standard deviation of this data is:
question: If the data <math><msub><mrow><mi>x</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow></msub><mo>,</mo><msub><mrow><mi>x</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub><mo>,</mo><mo>…</mo><msub><mrow><mi>x</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>10</mn></mrow></msub></math> is such that the mean of first four of these is <math><mn>11</mn><mo>,</mo></math> the mean of the remaining six is <math><mn>16</mn></math> and the sum of squares of all of these is <math><mn>2000</mn></math>, then the standard deviation of this data is:
solution:
Let the numbers are x1,x2,…x10N=10
Given x1+x2+x3+x4=4.11=44x5+x6….+x10=6.16=96⇒∑xi=140
⇒  variance σ2=∑xi2N-∑xiN2
⇒σ2=200010-140102
⇒σ2=200-196=4
⇒  Standard deviation σ=2.

subject: Mathematics
q: In a series of $2 n$ observations, half of them equal a and remaining half equal $-a$. If the standard deviation of the observations is 2 , then $|a|$ equals
question: In a series of $2 n$ observations, half of them equal a and remaining half equal $-a$. If the standard deviation of the observations is 2 , then $|a|$ equals
solution:
$x_i=a$ for $i=1,2, \ldots, n$ and $x_i=-a$ for $i=n, \ldots ., 2 n$
S.D. $=\sqrt{\frac{1}{2 n} \sum_{i=1}^{2 n}\left(x_i-\bar{x}\right)^2} \Rightarrow 2=\sqrt{\frac{1}{2 n} \sum_{i=1}^{2 n} x_i^2} \quad\left(\right.$ Since $\left.\sum_{i=1}^{2 n} x_i=0\right) \Rightarrow 2=\sqrt{\frac{1}{2 n} \cdot 2 n a^2} \Rightarrow|a|=2$

q:
If for some x∈R, the frequency distribution of the marks obtained by 20 students in a test is:

Marks 2 3 5 7
Frequency distribution x+12 2x-5 x2-3x x

Then the mean of the marks is :
question:
If for some x∈R, the frequency distribution of the marks obtained by 20 students in a test is:

Marks 2 3 5 7
Frequency distribution x+12 2x-5 x2-3x x

Then the mean of the marks is :
solution:
Total students =20

⇒  x+12+2x-5+x2-3x+x=20

⇒   x2+2x+1+2x-5+x2-3x+x=20

⇒   2x2+2x-24=0  ⇒  x2+x-12=0

⇒    x=-4, 3    ∵  x>0

∴    x=3

Average =x+12×2+2x-53+5x2-3x+7×x20

=32+3+0+2120=2.8

Hence, required mean is 2.8

subject: Mathematics
q: A set of four observations has mean $1$ and variance $13$. Another set of six observations has mean $2$ and variance $1$. Then, the variance of all these $10$ observations is equal to:
question: A set of four observations has mean $1$ and variance $13$. Another set of six observations has mean $2$ and variance $1$. Then, the variance of all these $10$ observations is equal to:
solution:
Given for the first set: $n_1 = 4$, $\bar{x}_1 = 1$, $\sigma_1^2 = 13$.

$\sigma_1^2 = \dfrac{\sum x_i^2}{n_1} - (\bar{x}_1)^2$

$13 = \dfrac{\sum x_i^2}{4} - 1^2 \Rightarrow \sum x_i^2 = 56$

Also, $\sum x_i = n_1 \bar{x}_1 = 4 \times 1 = 4$

Given for the second set: $n_2 = 6$, $\bar{x}_2 = 2$, $\sigma_2^2 = 1$.

$\sigma_2^2 = \dfrac{\sum y_i^2}{n_2} - (\bar{x}_2)^2$

$1 = \dfrac{\sum y_i^2}{6} - 2^2 \Rightarrow \sum y_i^2 = 30$

Also, $\sum y_i = n_2 \bar{x}_2 = 6 \times 2 = 12$

For the combined set of $10$ observations:

Total sum $\sum z_i = \sum x_i + \sum y_i = 4 + 12 = 16$

Combined mean $\bar{z} = \dfrac{16}{10} = 1.6$

Total sum of squares $\sum z_i^2 = \sum x_i^2 + \sum y_i^2 = 56 + 30 = 86$

Combined variance $\sigma^2 = \dfrac{\sum z_i^2}{10} - (\bar{z})^2$

$\sigma^2 = \dfrac{86}{10} - (1.6)^2 = 8.6 - 2.56 = 6.04$

Answer: $6.04$

question: The mean of a data set consisting of 20 observations is 40 . If one observation 53 was wrongly recorded as 33 , then the correct mean will be:
solution:
$$
\text { Correct mean }=\frac{20 \times 40-33+55}{20}=41.1
$$
Nearest option : (a) 41

q: If the mean and standard deviation of <math><mn>5</mn></math> observations <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>x</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><msub><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mo>,</mo><mi> </mi><msub><mi>x</mi><mn>3</mn></msub><mo>,</mo><mi> </mi><msub><mi>x</mi><mn>4</mn></msub><mo>,</mo><mi> </mi><msub><mi>x</mi><mn>5</mn></msub></math> are <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn></math> and <math><mn>3</mn><mo>,</mo></math> respectively, then the variance of <math><mn>6</mn></math> observations <math><msub><mrow><mi>x</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow></msub><mo>,</mo><mi> </mi><msub><mrow><mi>x</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub><mo>,</mo><mi> </mi><mo>…</mo><mo>,</mo><mi> </mi><msub><mrow><mi>x</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>5</mn></mrow></msub></math> and <math><mo>-</mo><mn>50</mn></math> is equal to

=545+25006=30456=507.5

q: The mean and standard deviation of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn></math> observations are <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>8</mn></math> respectively. Later on, it was observed that one observation was recorded as <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>50</mn></math> instead of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>40</mn></math>. Then the correct variance is
question: The mean and standard deviation of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn></math> observations are <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>8</mn></math> respectively. Later on, it was observed that one observation was recorded as <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>50</mn></math> instead of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>40</mn></math>. Then the correct variance is
solution:
Incorrect mean is

∑i=110xi10=20

⇒∑i=110xi=200   ....i

So, corrected mean is

∑i=110xi-50+4010=200-1010=19

Incorrect variance is

∑i=110xi210-400=64

⇒∑i=110xi2=4640

Now, corrected variance is

=∑i=19xi2+402-50210-192

=4640-90010-192=374-361=13

subject: Mathematics
q: Consider a set of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mi>n</mi></math> numbers having variance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo>.</mo></math> In this set, the mean of first <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mi>n</mi></math> numbers is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6</mn></math> and the mean of the remaining <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>n</mi></math> numbers is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo>.</mo></math> A new set is constructed by adding <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn></math> into each of the first <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mi>n</mi></math> numbers, and subtracting <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn></math> from each of the remaining <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>n</mi></math> numbers. If the variance of the new set is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>k</mi><mo>,</mo></math> then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>9</mn><mi>k</mi></math> is equal to ______.
question: Consider a set of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mi>n</mi></math> numbers having variance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo>.</mo></math> In this set, the mean of first <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mi>n</mi></math> numbers is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6</mn></math> and the mean of the remaining <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>n</mi></math> numbers is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo>.</mo></math> A new set is constructed by adding <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn></math> into each of the first <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mi>n</mi></math> numbers, and subtracting <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn></math> from each of the remaining <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>n</mi></math> numbers. If the variance of the new set is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>k</mi><mo>,</mo></math> then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>9</mn><mi>k</mi></math> is equal to ______.
solution:
Let, the number be a1, a2, a3,..., a2n, b1, b2, b3,..., bn.

Given, the mean of the first 2n numbers is 6 and that of the remaining n numbers is 3, then by using the formula for combined mean, we get, the mean of the complete set as μ=2n×6+n×32n+n=15n3n=5.

Also, the variance is defined as σ2=∑xi2n-μ2

4=∑ai2+∑bi23n-(5)2

⇒∑ai2+∑bi23n=29

⇒∑ai2+∑bi2=87n   ...i

Now, as per the given information, the distribution becomes

a1+1, a2+1, a3+1,..., a2n+1, b1-1, b2-1,..., bn-1

Thus, the new variance is

k=∑(ai+1)2+∑(bi-1)23n-12n+2n+3n-n3n2

⇒k=∑ai2+2n+2∑ai+∑bi2+n-2∑bi3n-1632

⇒k=∑ai2+∑bi2+3n+2∑ai-2∑bi3n-1632

On putting the given values and value from the equation i, 

⇒k=87n+3n+2(12n)-2(3n)3n-1632

⇒k=1083-1632

⇒9k=3(108)-(16)2

⇒9k=324-256=68.

subject: Mathematics
q: Let $\mathrm{a}, \mathrm{b}, \mathrm{c} \in \mathrm{N}$ and $\mathrm{a} < \mathrm{b} < \mathrm{c}$. Let the mean, the mean deviation about the mean and the variance of the 5 observations $9,25, \mathrm{a}, \mathrm{b}, \mathrm{c}$ be 18,4 and $\frac{136}{5}$, respectively. Then $2 \mathrm{a}+\mathrm{b}-\mathrm{c}$ is equal to__________
question: Let $\mathrm{a}, \mathrm{b}, \mathrm{c} \in \mathrm{N}$ and $\mathrm{a} < \mathrm{b} < \mathrm{c}$. Let the mean, the mean deviation about the mean and the variance of the 5 observations $9,25, \mathrm{a}, \mathrm{b}, \mathrm{c}$ be 18,4 and $\frac{136}{5}$, respectively. Then $2 \mathrm{a}+\mathrm{b}-\mathrm{c}$ is equal to__________
solution:
$a, b, c \in N \quad a < b < c$
$\overline{\mathrm{x}}=$ mean $=\frac{9+25+\mathrm{a}+\mathrm{b}+\mathrm{c}}{5}=18$
$a+b+c=56$
Mean deviation $=\frac{\Sigma\left|\mathrm{x}_{\mathrm{i}}-\overline{\mathrm{x}}\right|}{\mathrm{n}}=4$
$\begin{aligned} & =9+7+|18-\mathrm{a}|+|18-\mathrm{b}|+|18-\mathrm{c}|=20 \\ & =|18-\mathrm{a}|+|18-\mathrm{b}|+|18-\mathrm{c}|=4\end{aligned}$
Variance $=\frac{\Sigma\left|\mathrm{x}_{\mathrm{i}}-\overline{\mathrm{x}}\right|^2}{\mathrm{n}}=\frac{136}{5}$
$\begin{aligned} & =81+49+|18-a|^2+|18-b|^2+|18-c|^2=136 \\ & =(18-a)^2+(18-b)^2+(18-c)^2=6\end{aligned}$
Possible values $(18-\mathrm{a})^2=1, \quad(18-\mathrm{b})^2=1 \quad(18-\mathrm{c})^2=4$ $\mathrm{a} < \mathrm{b} < \mathrm{c}$
so $\quad \begin{array}{ccc}18-a=1 & 18-b=-1 & 18-c=-2 \\ \mathrm{a}=17 & \mathrm{~b}=19 & \mathrm{c}=20\end{array}$
$a+b+c=56$
$2 \mathrm{a}+\mathrm{b}-\mathrm{c} 34=19-20=33$

question: Let the observation <math><msub><mrow><mi>x</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>i</mi></mrow></msub><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo>≤</mo><mi>i</mi><mo>≤</mo><mn>10</mn></mrow></mfenced></math> satisfy the equations  <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><munderover><mo>∑</mo><mrow><mi>i</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow><mn>10</mn></munderover><mfenced><mrow><msub><mi>x</mi><mi>i</mi></msub><mo>-</mo><mn>5</mn></mrow></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mn>10</mn></math>, <math><mrow><munderover><mo stretchy="false">∑</mo><mrow><mi>i</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow><mrow><mn>10</mn></mrow></munderover><mrow><msup><mrow><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><msub><mrow><mi>x</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>i</mi></mrow></msub><mo>-</mo><mn>5</mn></mrow></mfenced></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo>=</mo><mn>40</mn></mrow></mrow></math> . If <math><mi>μ</mi></math> and <math><mi>λ</mi></math> are the mean and the variance of the observations, <math><msub><mrow><mi>x</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow></msub><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn><mo>,</mo><mi></mi><msub><mrow><mi>x</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn><mo>,</mo><mi></mi><mo>.</mo><mi></mi><mi></mi><mo>.</mo><mi></mi><mi></mi><mo>.</mo><mi></mi><mi></mi><mo>.</mo><mo>,</mo><msub><mrow><mi>x</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>10</mn></mrow></msub><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn><mo>,</mo></math> then the ordered pair <math><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>μ</mi><mo>,</mo><mi>λ</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> is equal to:
solution:
Mean xi-5=∑xi-510=1 ∴Meanxi-5+2=3
μ=varxi-5=∑xi-5210-∑xi-510=3

q: If the mean and variance of five observations are <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mn>24</mn><mn>5</mn></mfrac></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mn>194</mn><mn>25</mn></mfrac></math> respectively and the mean of first four observations is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mn>7</mn><mn>2</mn></mfrac></math>, then the variance of the first four observations in equal to
question: If the mean and variance of five observations are <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mn>24</mn><mn>5</mn></mfrac></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mn>194</mn><mn>25</mn></mfrac></math> respectively and the mean of first four observations is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mn>7</mn><mn>2</mn></mfrac></math>, then the variance of the first four observations in equal to
solution:
Given: 

x=245 and variance is 19425

⇒∑i=15xi=245×5

⇒∑i=15xi=24

Also, variance

⇒∑i=15xi25-2452=19425

⇒∑i=15xi2=154

Now, 5th observation =24-sum of first 4 terms

=24-72×4=10

So, new variance =∑i=14xi24-722

=154-1004-494

=54

q: In a class of 100 students there are 70 boys whose average marks in a subject are 75. If the average marks of the complete class is 72, then what is the average of the girls?
question: In a class of 100 students there are 70 boys whose average marks in a subject are 75. If the average marks of the complete class is 72, then what is the average of the girls?
solution:
Total student $=100 ;$ for 70 stds $75 \times 70=5250 \Rightarrow 7200-5250=1950$
$$
\text { Average of girls }=\frac{1950}{30}=65
$$

subject: Mathematics
q: A data consists of $20$ observations $x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_{20}$. If $\sum_{i=1}^{20}(x_i + 5)^2 = 2500$ and $\sum_{i=1}^{20}(x_i - 5)^2 = 100$, then the ratio of mean to standard deviation of this data is:
question: A data consists of $20$ observations $x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_{20}$. If $\sum_{i=1}^{20}(x_i + 5)^2 = 2500$ and $\sum_{i=1}^{20}(x_i - 5)^2 = 100$, then the ratio of mean to standard deviation of this data is:
solution:
Given $\sum_{i=1}^{20}(x_i + 5)^2 = 2500$ and $\sum_{i=1}^{20}(x_i - 5)^2 = 100$.

Expanding the first equation:
$\sum_{i=1}^{20} (x_i^2 + 10x_i + 25) = 2500$
$\sum_{i=1}^{20} x_i^2 + 10 \sum_{i=1}^{20} x_i + 500 = 2500$
$\sum_{i=1}^{20} x_i^2 + 10 \sum_{i=1}^{20} x_i = 2000$ --- (1)

Expanding the second equation:
$\sum_{i=1}^{20} (x_i^2 - 10x_i + 25) = 100$
$\sum_{i=1}^{20} x_i^2 - 10 \sum_{i=1}^{20} x_i + 500 = 100$
$\sum_{i=1}^{20} x_i^2 - 10 \sum_{i=1}^{20} x_i = -400$ --- (2)

Subtracting (2) from (1):
$20 \sum_{i=1}^{20} x_i = 2400 \Rightarrow \sum_{i=1}^{20} x_i = 120$

Adding (1) and (2):
$2 \sum_{i=1}^{20} x_i^2 = 1600 \Rightarrow \sum_{i=1}^{20} x_i^2 = 800$

Mean ($\mu$) is given by:
$\mu = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{20} x_i}{20} = \dfrac{120}{20} = 6$

Variance ($\sigma^2$) is given by:
$\sigma^2 = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{20} x_i^2}{20} - \mu^2 = \dfrac{800}{20} - 6^2 = 40 - 36 = 4$

Standard deviation ($\sigma$) is $\sqrt{4} = 2$.

The ratio of mean to standard deviation is:
$\dfrac{\mu}{\sigma} = \dfrac{6}{2} = \dfrac{3}{1}$

Answer: $3:1$

subject: Mathematics
q: For two data sets, each of size 5 , the variances are given to be 4 and 5 and the corresponding means are given to be 2 and 4 , respectively. The variance of the combined data set is
question: For two data sets, each of size 5 , the variances are given to be 4 and 5 and the corresponding means are given to be 2 and 4 , respectively. The variance of the combined data set is
solution:
$\sigma_x^2=4$
$\sigma_y^2=5$
$\bar{x}=2$
$\bar{y}=4$
$\begin{aligned} \frac{\sum x_i}{5} & =2 \\ \sigma_x^2 & =\left(\frac{1}{2} \sum x_i^2\right)-(\bar{x})^2=\frac{1}{5}\left(\sum y_i^2\right)-16 \\ \sum x_i^2 & =40 \\ \sum y_i^2 & =105 \\ \sigma_z^2 & =\frac{1}{10}\left(\sum x_i^2+\sum y_i^2\right)-\left(\frac{\bar{x}+\bar{y}}{2}\right)^2=\frac{1}{10}(40+105)-9=\frac{145-90}{10}=\frac{55}{10}=\frac{11}{2}\end{aligned}$

subject: Mathematics
q: Let the mean of 6 observations <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>2</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>4</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>5</mn><mo>,</mo><mi>x</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>y</mi></math> be <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn></math> and their variance be <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn></math>. Then their mean deviation about the mean is equal to
question: Let the mean of 6 observations <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>2</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>4</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>5</mn><mo>,</mo><mi>x</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>y</mi></math> be <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn></math> and their variance be <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn></math>. Then their mean deviation about the mean is equal to
solution:
Consider the data 1,2,4,5,x,y.

So,

Mean=1+2+4+5+x+y6

⇒5=x+y+126

⇒x+y=18

And,

Variance=∑xi2n-∑xin2

⇒10=12+22+42+52+x2+y26-52

⇒x2+y2=164

We have x+y=18 and x2+y2=164.

Now solving above equations we get, x=10, y=8,

Now mean deviation about mean will be =∑i=16xi-x6

=1-5+2-5+4-5+5-5+10-5+8-56

=166

=83

Therefore, the required answer is 83

subject: Mathematics
q: The mean and variance of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>7</mn></math> observations are <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>8</mn></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>16</mn></math> respectively. If one observation <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>14</mn></math> is omitted, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>a</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>b</mi></math> are respectively mean and variance of remaining <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6</mn></math> observation, then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>a</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>3</mn><mo> </mo><mi>b</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>5</mn></math> is equal to ________
question: The mean and variance of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>7</mn></math> observations are <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>8</mn></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>16</mn></math> respectively. If one observation <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>14</mn></math> is omitted, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>a</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>b</mi></math> are respectively mean and variance of remaining <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6</mn></math> observation, then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>a</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>3</mn><mo> </mo><mi>b</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>5</mn></math> is equal to ________
solution:
Given,

Mean and variance of 7 observation is given as 8 & 16 respectively,

So using the formula of mean we get, x¯=S7⇒S=8×7=56

Now if we remove 14 sum will be S=56-14=42 and new mean will be a=426=7

Now using the formula of variance we get,

∑i=16xi2+1427-x¯2=16

⇒∑i=16xi2+1427-82=16

⇒∑i=16xi2+1427=80

⇒∑i=16xi2+142=560

⇒∑i=16xi=364

Now, new variance b=∑i=16xi26-a2=3646-72=353

Hence, the value of a+3b-5=7+35-5=37

subject: Mathematics
q:
Let M denote the median of the following frequency distribution.

 Class 0-44-88-1212-1616-20 Frequency 391086

Then 20M is equal to :
question:
Let M denote the median of the following frequency distribution.

 Class 0-44-88-1212-1616-20 Frequency 391086

Then 20M is equal to :
solution:
\(\begin{array}{|c|c|c|} \hline \text {Class } & \text {Frequency } & \text {Cumulative frequency } \\ \hline 0-4 & 3 & 3 \\ \hline 4-8 & 9 & 12 \\ \hline 8-12 & 10 & 22 \\ \hline 12-16 & 8 & 30 \\ \hline 16-20 & 6 & 36 \\ \hline \end{array}\)

M=l+N2-Cfh

Here, N=3+9+10+8+6=36

⇒N2=18

So, median class is 8-12

⇒l=8, C=22, f=10, h=4

⇒M=8+18-1210×4

⇒M=10.4

⇒20 M =208

subject: Mathematics
q: The mean and the variance of five observations are <math><mn>4</mn></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5.20</mn></math>, respectively. If three of the observations are <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>4</mn></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn></math>; then the absolute value of the difference of the other two observations, is :
question: The mean and the variance of five observations are <math><mn>4</mn></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5.20</mn></math>, respectively. If three of the observations are <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>4</mn></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn></math>; then the absolute value of the difference of the other two observations, is :
solution:
Let rest two observations are x and y.

Given mean =4

⇒3+4+4+x+y5=4⇒x+y=9     ...1

Given variance =5.2

⇒9+16+16+x2+y25-42=5.2

⇒x2+y2=65     ...2

Squaring equation 1 and subtracting equation 2, we get

2xy=81-65⇒2xy=16⇒xy=8

∴ x-y2=x2+y2-2xy=65-16=49

∴ x-y=7
